# Concepts and Principles of Metals and Alloys


1. Metals play a crucial role in engineering, manufacturing, and design.
2. Understanding their **extraction, processing, properties, and sustainability** allows designers to select the best materials for their intended use.

## Extracting Metal from Ore


1. Metals are **not found in pure form** in nature but are typically combined with other elements in **rock formations called ores**. 
  1. Extracting these metals is a **complex and energy-intensive** process.
2. **Ore deposits are localized** → Some regions have rich metal reserves, while others rely on imports.
3. **Metals may be mined in one location** but refined elsewhere, requiring transportation and logistics.

<callout type="note">1. Designers must **consider the environmental impact** of metal extraction.
2. Choosing recyclable materials or **reducing metal waste** can improve sustainability.</callout>

![Yes, we actually used Minecraft as an example](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c684cc4ae7a763d4db5e387125c56a12c47aa3a8-300x300.png)



### Grain Size and Metal Properties


1. Metals have **crystalline structures** made up of grains, and the **size of these grains** influences their **mechanical properties**.
  1. **Rapid cooling → Small grains → Harder, stronger material**.
  2. **Slow cooling → Large grains → More ductile, less strong material**.

![Metal grain size and characteristics](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/deabd483deaf180ebe7c8c8cc5abf8a9e56d8fc3-960x444.png)

<callout type="note">1. **Aircraft parts** require fine-grained metals for strength.
2. **Steel beams** may be engineered with controlled grain size for balance between strength and flexibility.</callout>



### Modifying Metal Properties


Metals can be **engineered for improved performance** through different processes.

1. **Alloying**
  1. **What It Is:** Mixing a metal with other elements to **enhance strength, corrosion resistance, and durability**.
  2. **Examples:**
      1. **Stainless Steel (Iron + Chromium)** → Resists corrosion, used in cutlery and medical instruments.
    2. **Brass (Copper + Zinc)** → Used in plumbing and musical instruments.
2. **Work Hardening & Tempering**
  1. **Work Hardening:** Increases hardness by **compressing grains** without heating (e.g., cold rolling).
  2. **Tempering:** Applies heat **after work hardening** to improve toughness and reduce brittleness.
  3. **Examples:**
      1. **Knives & razors** → Tempering ensures long-lasting sharpness.
    2. **Industrial springs & tools** → Work hardening prevents deformation.

![Common alloys](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/2a694c3c41c9c1391c8add6cdeaf2721aeeacf45-1999x1396.png)



![Tempering](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/efd4d77b51e2dd67a803e3c8d196579a76b628d8-700x700.png)



### Super Alloys: Engineered for Extreme Conditions


1. Super alloys are **high-performance metal alloys** designed to withstand **high temperatures, corrosion, and mechanical stress**.
  1. **Key Properties:**
      1. High **creep resistance** (resistance to slow deformation under heat).
    2. High **oxidation and corrosion resistance**.

<callout type="example">1. **Jet engines** must resist high heat and pressure.
2. **Spacecraft components** need super alloys for durability in extreme environments.</callout>



![Aerospace Superalloys](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/08a7effdafa68d76344da0e280903b321ac49d17-591x366.png)

****

### Sustainability Considerations


#### Metal Recycling


1. **Metals can be indefinitely recycled** without losing quality.
2. Recycling uses **less energy than mining** new metals, reducing environmental impact.



#### Design for Disassembly (DfD)


1. **What It Is:** Designing products to be easily disassembled for **recycling and repairs**.
2. **Examples:**
  1. **Phones & laptops** → Modular components make repairs easier.
  2. **Automotive parts** → Designed for easy separation and material recovery.



## Types of Metals and Alloys


Metals are classified as

1. **Ferrous (contain iron)**
2. **Non-ferrous (do not contain iron)**.

### Ferrous Metals (Contain Iron, Magnetic**)**


| Metal | Common uses |
| --- | --- |
| Mild Steel | Car bodies, construction, nuts, bolts, screws |
| Stainless Steel | Cutlery, kitchen surfaces, medical tools |
| Cast Iron | Machinery, clamps, engine blocks |



### Non-Ferrous Metals (Do Not Contain Iron, Non-Magnetic)


| Metal | Common Uses |
| --- | --- |
| Aluminum | Drink cans, aircraft parts, kitchen foil |
| Copper | Electrical wiring, plumbing, printed circuit boards |
| Silver/Tin | Soldering, jewelry, coatings |
| Zinc | Galvanizing (protective coating for steel) |



### Common Alloys


| Alloy | Composition | Common Uses |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Brass | Copper + Zinc | Plumbing fittings, musical instruments |
| High-Speed Steel (HSS) | Iron + Copper + Other elements | Drill bits, cutting tools |



<callout type="note">#### Bottom Line: Why This Matters for Designers


1. **Material Selection Impacts Performance** → Understanding grain size, alloys, and treatments ensures materials meet design needs.
2. **Super Alloys Enable Innovation** → Used in **high-performance applications like aerospace, medical, and automotive**.
3. **Sustainability is Key** → **Recycling and design for disassembly** reduce environmental impact.
4. **Ferrous vs. Non-Ferrous Metals** → Each category has specific uses based on **strength, corrosion resistance, and weight**.</callout>
